Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Or Dismiss Proceedings
Off Calendar Jul-28-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Dow W. Patten of the law offices of Smith Pattern (415.402.0084) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Collin D. Cook of the law offices of Fisher Phillips (415.490.9032) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT VENDINI, INC., INSPERITY PEO SERVICES, L.P.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Or Dismiss Proceedings

Defendants Vendini, Inc. and Insperity PEO Services, L.P.'s motion to compel arbitration and stay of this case pending the conclusion of the arbitration is granted. Defendants establish that both Vendini and Ms. Moore agreed to arbitrate. While Vendini did not formally "sign" the arbitration agreement, it presented the offer letter that included the agreement to Ms. Moore and Vendini's Human Resources Manager Constance Haddad initialed the agreement on Vendini's behalf. (Haddad Dec. par. 14.) Mutual assent is determined under an objective standard and the only reasonable inference from the parties' conduct is that both agreed to arbitrate their disputes. Ms. Moore fails to demonstrate that the agreement is unconscionable. Ms. Moore complains that the agreement was a contract of adhesion. Ms. Moore, however, fails to provide an opposing declaration and her title of Director of Security & Compliance and her compensation suggest that she had more ability to negotiate the terms of her employment than would a lower level employee. Ms. Moore notes that the arbitration agreement requires AAA or JAMS arbitration and those rules were not provided to her. The failure to provide the rules is not problematic given that Ms. Moore could have obtained a copy by accessing the internet and her claim of unconscionability is not based on the substance of the rules. (Lane v. Francis Capital Management LLC (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 676, 691-692; Farrar v. Direct Commerce, Inc. (2017) 9 Cal.App.5th 1257, 1268.) Ms. Moore complains that the arbitration agreement carves out misappropriation claims and an employer is more likely to prosecute such claims. Even assuming that such a carve out is substantively unconscionable, it can be severed. (Farrar, 9 Cal.App.5th at 1273 - 1276.) Ms. Moore states that she may be forced to pay extra fees. The agreement does not state that Ms. Moore shall bear the arbitration fees and Vendini has agreed to pay such fees. Ms. Moore also states that unconscionability exists because there is no assurance of a written award. That argument fails because both AAA and JAMS rules provide for a written award. (AAA Employment Arbitration Rule 39(c); JAMS Employment Arbitration Rule 24(h).) Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org. (D302)
